transmission.detail: 20d net bills; heavy supply absorbs MMF cash, widens vs OIS
20d net Notes+Bonds; duration supply pressures term premium

narrative.regime_text: Government spending is outpacing receipts at an improving rate. This mechanically adds reserves and tends to coincide with easier front-end funding.

lane_decomposition.caveat: Composition observation only. Pre-2023-02-14 the Treasury commingled withheld FICA and corporate income tax in a single FTD bucket; lane split is clean from FY2024 onward. Lane signal has no forward equity edge: see dead-ends/dts_project.md.

rules.entry: T+10 trading days after first cluster day

macro_setups.caveat: Regime indicators (exposure tilts), not discrete trade triggers. Historical bucket stats are unconditional 120d forward SPX averages across 5,000+ trading days; entering at the start of a fresh regime may not capture the full edge. Use alongside Tier-1 timing system.

cr_status.message: No full-year appropriations found. Government operating under CR.

report.framework: This report applies an operational lens consistent with the accounting of the Federal Reserve and the US Treasury as described in the Daily Treasury Statement. Government spending = the Treasury instructs the Fed to credit reserve accounts (dollars created). Taxes and tariffs = the Fed debits reserve accounts (dollars destroyed). Securities issuance = the Fed debits reserve accounts and credits securities accounts (reserve drain, not borrowing). Securities redemption = the Fed debits securities accounts and credits reserve accounts (reserve add). The deficit = net dollars added to the non-government sector = the non-government sector's net savings in dollar financial assets. The TGA = a timing buffer between spending and collection, not a constraint on the government's ability to spend.
report.executive_summary: As of 2026-06-05, the fiscal-flow regime reads **Supportive** for the non-government sector. Net fiscal transfer momentum is **accelerating**: 5d average is $30.2bn versus prior-20d average $15.6bn. MTD net transfers are $151.2bn, with YoY comparable at +341.9%. FYTD, the government has added $1,123.1bn in net financial assets to the non-government sector (+3.6% YoY); this is the net dollars spent that have not yet been used to pay taxes or purchase securities. The TGA closed at $825.5bn. Treasury spending has exceeded collections recently, drawing down the TGA and crediting reserves to the banking system.
report.what_changed: Over the last 5 sessions, average net fiscal transfer was $30.2bn per day, versus $15.6bn in the prior 20-session baseline. Tax drains averaged $17.6bn versus $16.5bn, while net withdrawals averaged $52.0bn against $36.4bn. The largest daily move in the 20-day window was 2026-06-01 at $109.6bn. On balance, weekly flow momentum is improving relative to the recent baseline.
scorecard_items: MTD net fiscal transfer (spending minus taxes/tariffs): $151.2bn (+341.9% YoY comparable).
FYTD net financial assets added to non-government sector: $1,123.1bn (+3.6% YoY comparable).
MTD government spending (reserve credits): $260.0bn vs MTD tax/tariff drains (reserve debits): $108.7bn.
MTD household transfers (dollars created â†' households): $55.0bn (-60.1% YoY).
MTD interest income to bondholders (dollars created â†' savers): $25.1bn (-46.9% YoY).
MTD tax refunds (dollars returned â†' households): $4.6bn (-32.4% YoY).
report.drivers: Relative to the comparable month last year, the shift is primarily spend-led. Government spending (reserve credits) moved by $-266.1bn while tax and tariff drains (reserve debits) moved by $-479.9bn. The FYTD operating deficit; dollars spent by the government that have not yet been used to pay taxes. is $1,123.1bn, versus $1,084.3bn at this point last year. This is identically the net financial assets added to the non-government sector's savings. Tariff collections are running +88.8% above prior year at $231.5bn FYTD, now representing 5.1% of total deposit drains. Tariffs are operationally identical to taxes: they destroy dollars. When the government collects a tariff, the Fed debits the importer's bank's reserve account; those dollars cease to exist in the private sector. Monthly tariff drains are approximately 0.17x the monthly SSA payment cycle. While tariffs are not yet cancelling out SSA payments in aggregate, the daily tariff collection trend is creeping higher and worth monitoring. both are direct dollar flows between the government and households, in opposite directions. Interest payments to bondholders are running at approximately $681.9bn on a trailing 12-month basis, representing 8.7% of total government spending. This is one of the largest single fiscal injection channels: when Treasury pays interest, the Fed credits reserve accounts of bondholder banks; these are new dollars entering the private sector. As the white paper describes, a positive policy rate is effectively "basic income for those who already have money." The higher the debt/GDP, the larger the fiscal impact of rate policy. This creates a paradox: what the Fed calls "tightening" simultaneously injects more dollars into the economy through the interest income channel, partially offsetting the intended demand reduction. Total FYTD government spending is -2.1% versus prior year, broadly on a similar trajectory to FY2025 despite headlines about spending cuts. The aggregate flow data does not yet show a material reduction in the rate at which the government is crediting reserve accounts.

signals.title: Government Account Series Divergence
signals.text: Net GAS issuance is $303.0bn vs $-149.3bn at this point last year (+302.9%). Large shifts in GAS often indicate extraordinary measures during debt ceiling episodes, or significant trust fund rebalancing. This divergence warrants attention in the context of any upcoming debt ceiling deadlines.

signals.title: Tariff Revenue Acceleration
signals.text: Tariff/customs receipts FYTD: $231.5bn (+88.8% YoY). Tariffs now represent 5.1% of total deposit drains. Operationally, tariffs are identical to taxes: when collected, the Fed debits the importer's bank's reserve account; those dollars are destroyed. The economy needs the government to spend or lend those dollars back into existence before they can circulate again. The DTS does not distinguish policy tariff changes from routine customs flows, but the magnitude of the YoY shift is consistent with the new tariff regime taking effect, directly reducing private-sector dollar balances.

signals.title: Interest Income Channel & Rate Paradox
signals.text: Trailing 12-month interest payments to bondholders: approximately $681.9bn, representing 8.7% of all government spending. When Treasury pays coupon interest, the Fed credits reserve accounts; this is new money entering the economy, functionally identical to any other government spending. At current policy rates, this channel operates as what Warren Mosler describes as "basic income for those who already have money." The paradox: what the Fed calls "tightening" simultaneously increases this fiscal injection. The higher the debt/GDP ratio, the larger the fiscal impact of rate policy. If rates remain elevated and the debt continues to roll at higher coupons, interest expense will continue climbing toward and potentially beyond $1 trillion annually. a massive, regressive fiscal stimulus running on autopilot through the interest income channel.

report.base_case: The current supportive regime persists, with net transfers running above baseline. This setup adds reserves to the banking system and is more likely to leave growth and liquidity modestly boosted than constrained.
report.alt_scenario: Conditions deteriorate if withdrawals slow while receipts hold steady, pushing 5d net-transfer averages below 20d averages. Confirmation of the supportive read would be continued positive MTD and FYTD YoY deltas plus repeated net-transfer prints above the 20d baseline.
report.falsification: Sustained negative YoY net-transfer prints alongside falling 5d averages below the 20d baseline for more than 3 consecutive sessions.

market_map.reason: Supportive fiscal flow + accelerating momentum. Government spending is crediting more reserves to the banking system than taxes are draining; historically positive for risk assets.

Heavy 20d net bill issuance ($179.1bn). When Treasury issues bills, the Fed debits reserve accounts and credits securities accounts; draining reserves from the banking system. This pushes bill rates wider vs OIS as MMFs absorb supply.

Heavy 20d coupon issuance ($135.3bn). Notes and bonds drain reserves from the banking system and add duration to private portfolios. This is not the government "borrowing"; it is a reserve drain that occurs after spending has already credited reserves. But the term premium effect is real: more duration supply pushes yields higher.
Mixed fiscal signals; no strong directional USD read from flows
Reserve adds from both fiscal injection and TGA drawdown. When Treasury spends from the TGA, the Fed debits TGA and credits bank reserves; double support for funding conditions.

cot.narrative: S&P 500 leveraged fund positioning is at a **extreme short** with a z-score of -1.9 (2th percentile over 52 weeks). This is the high-conviction contrarian setup: supportive fiscal flows with the crowd positioned the wrong way. Leveraged funds are net short into an environment where the government is adding reserves to the banking system. 10Y Treasury leveraged funds are net short (z-score: 1.0). Crowded long duration; betting on rate cuts or recession. If fiscal injection remains elevated, the bond bulls may be disappointed.

margin.contrarian: Bearish contrarian signal — speculative leverage at extremes. Historically, margin debt rolling over from these levels precedes drawdowns.
margin.narrative: FINRA margin debt as of 2026-04 is $1304.3bn (+53.3% YoY, trend: rising). This is at **extreme leverage** levels (z-score: 1.70). Bearish contrarian signal — speculative leverage at extremes. Historically, margin debt rolling over from these levels precedes drawdowns. Elevated speculative leverage coinciding with supportive fiscal flows. the crowd is already positioned. The fiscal tailwind is priced in via margin.

fred_refs.title: Federal Government Current Expenditures: Interest Payments
fred_refs.reason: Interest income channel running ~$681.9bn/yr; the largest autopilot fiscal injection to the saver class. Track proximity to $1T.

fred_refs.title: Treasury General Account Balance
fred_refs.reason: TGA at $825.5bn. spending exceeding collections (reserve add). TGA is the timing buffer between government spending and collection.

fred_refs.title: Customs Duties Revenue

fred_refs.reason: Heavy bill issuance ($179.1bn in 20d) draining reserves; check if front-end rates widening as banks compete for reduced reserve balances.

fred_refs.title: Federal Debt: Total Public Debt
fred_refs.reason: Total outstanding public debt = total dollars spent by government not yet used to pay taxes. Context for GAS/extraordinary measures and the interest income channel scale.

fred_refs.title: FINRA Margin Debt. Debit Balances in Margin Accounts
fred_refs.reason: FINRA margin debt at $1304.3bn (z-score: 1.70); speculative leverage is elevated vs recent history.

caveats: Seasonal tax dates, refunds, and benefit cycles create temporary volatility in daily flow reads.
Current month is partial until month end, so MTD inferences are provisional.
FYTD reads are matched by fiscal month index for apples-to-apples YoY comparisons.
Tariff/customs categories include broader customs receipts, not only policy tariff changes; but operationally they all destroy dollars identically.
Interest payments add dollars to the private sector in aggregate, but transmission to spending demand is distribution-dependent; bondholders have a higher savings propensity than transfer recipients.

The public debt is not a liability in the household sense. It is the accounting record of net dollars the government has spent that have not yet been used to pay taxes; it equals the non-government sector's net savings of dollar financial assets.
Securities issuance is a reserve drain, not borrowing. The government spends first (credits reserves), then offers securities which debit reserves and credit securities accounts at the Fed.
report.policy_monitor:
• **Treasury bill auctions (26-week and 13-week) at 3.69% and 3.64% respectively) signal sustained near-term rollover demand and stable front-end funding costs.** DTS impact: securities issuance (reserve drain) proceeds smoothly with no sign of collateral stress or demand withdrawal; TGA rebuilds as bill proceeds flow in, mechanically draining reserves over 2–4 week settlement windows. Timing: imminent (issue date 06/11/2026); this week's DTS will show proceeds credited and auction-related trading activity in reserves.

• **Inflation expectations rising toward 4% this week (New York Fed household survey, bond market repricing) will pressure real interest outlays and wage-indexed transfer payments if CPI print exceeds consensus.** DTS impact: if realized, CPI-adjustment triggers on Social Security and other indexed benefits (household transfers line item) accelerate; simultaneously, higher inflation erodes TGA real purchasing power and may force earlier supplemental appropriations or accelerated draws. Timing: near-term (CPI data release mid-month); watch for elevated transfer-payment accruals beginning mid-June.

• **Iran military escalation rhetoric and oil-price volatility (currently cushioned by China below $100/bbl but analysts warn fragility) create tail risk to energy excise and customs receipt flows; extended conflict could spike oil above $120+ within weeks.** DTS impact: if sustained above $110/bbl, petroleum excise tax receipts (excises line item) and tariff-sensitive import duties accelerate upward; conversely, deflationary pressure from energy shock to consumer spending suppresses individual income and payroll tax receipts 4–8 weeks downstream. Timing: structural (price signal now, fiscal lag 30–60 days); no immediate DTS shift but monitor energy futures curve and weekly EIA inventory data for sustained shock signal.

• **Electoral calendar (Senate Budget Committee chair and multiple state-office races June 9) creates low near-term legislative risk to announced tax or spending policy; however, "NO TAX ON TIPS" rhetoric and border-security funding messaging signal post-election push for revenue-reducing or enforcement-spend provisions.** DTS impact: no immediate DTS consequence; watch for post-election legislative activity (mid-June onward) signaling proposed changes to payroll-tax withholding (tips income exclusion would reduce individual income-tax receipts) or ICE/CBP supplemental appropriations (household transfers, other mandatory or discretionary outlay lines). Timing: structural (post-June 9, legislative window opens); not yet reflected in DTS projections.
